elements.sent-2411-108: The Perfect Domination Problem (PDP), a classical challenge in combinatorial optimization, has significant applications in real-world scenarios, such as wireless and social networks.
elements.sent-2411-109: Over several decades of research, the problem has been demonstrated to be NP-complete across numerous graph classes.
elements.sent-2411-110: With the recent advancements in quantum computing, there has been a surge in the development of quantum algorithms aimed at addressing NP-complete problems, including the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA).
elements.sent-2411-111: However, the applicability of quantum algorithms to the PDP, as well as their efficacy in solving it, remains largely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-112: This study represents a pioneering effort to apply QAOA, with a limited number of layers, to address the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-113: Comprehensive testing and analysis were conducted across 420 distinct parameter combinations.
elements.sent-2411-114: Experimental results indicate that QAOA successfully identified the correct Perfect Dominating Set (PDS) in 82 cases, including 17 instances where the optimal PDS was computed.
elements.sent-2411-115: Furthermore, it was observed that with appropriate parameter settings, the approximation ratio could achieve a value close to 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-116: These findings underscore the potential of QAOA as a viable approach for solving the PDP, signifying an important milestone that introduces this problem into the realm of quantum computing.
elements.sent-2411-117: For a graph \( G = (V, E) \), a dominating set (DS) is a subset \( D \subseteq V \) such that every vertex \( v \in V \setminus D \) has at least one neighbor in \( D \).
elements.sent-2411-118: A more stringent requirement defines the perfect dominating set (PDS), where every vertex \( v \in V \setminus D \) must have exactly one neighbor in \( D \).
elements.sent-2411-119: The Perfect Domination Problem (PDP) seeks to identify the smallest such set \( D \).
elements.sent-2411-120: The PDP is closely related to various practical applications, including parallel computer networks \citep{RN482 , wireless communication networks \citep{RN483 , and social networks \citep{RN485 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: As a specialized variant of the domination problem (DP), the PDP has been proven NP-complete for many graph classes \citep{RN486, RN487 .}
elements.sent-2411-122: Over the years, numerous algorithms have been developed to address the PDP, often tailored to specific graph types or specialized problem variations, such as the weighted version, to manage its NP-complete nature.
elements.sent-2411-123: Significant studies have explored the PDP in various graph categories, including rectangular grid graphs \citep{RN488 , distance-hereditary graphs \citep{RN493 , circular-arc graphs \citep{RN490 , and knight’s graphs \citep{RN492 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-124: Additionally, the weighted PDP has been investigated in tree graphs \citep{RN487 , chordal graphs, and split graphs \citep{RN491 , while weighted independent PDP solutions have been studied for cocomparability graphs \citep{RN489 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-125: Despite some algorithms achieving polynomial or even linear time complexity for restricted graph types, solving the PDP remains computationally challenging in its general form due to its NP-complete nature.
elements.sent-2411-126: In recent years, the rapid advancement of quantum computing \citep{RN421, RN495, RN496, RN497 has spurred the development of numerous quantum algorithms aimed at addressing NP-complete combinatorial optimization problems.}
elements.sent-2411-127: Prominent among these algorithms are Quantum Annealing \citep{RN422 , the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) \citep{RN436 , and the Variational Quantum Eigensolver \citep{RN428 , among others.}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: As one of the most influential quantum algorithms of the past decade, QAOA has been applied to a wide array of combinatorial optimization problems, including max-cut \citep{RN436 , the traveling salesman problem \citep{RN453 , the domination problem (DP) \citep{RN415 , max-flow \citep{RN420 , the minimum vertex cover problem \citep{RN334 , the boolean satisfiability problem (SAT) \citep{RN455 , the independent domination problem \citep{RN498 , and the total domination problem \citep{RN499 , among others.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: In this study, we explore the application of QAOA to solve the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-130: Using a graph with 6 vertices, we conducted an extensive evaluation of low-layer QAOA across 420 distinct parameter combinations to assess its effectiveness in solving the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-131: The experimental results demonstrate that QAOA is capable of solving the PDP and exhibits significant potential for further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-132: By analyzing parameter distributions, we identified distinct trends in the approximation ratio and in the algorithm's ability to compute both the optimal and correct PDS.
elements.sent-2411-133: The primary contributions of this work are as follows: (1) This is the first study to apply a quantum algorithm to the PDP; (2) The results under various parameter settings reveal QAOA's tendencies when addressing the PDP, offering valuable insights and guidance for future research.
elements.sent-2411-134: The structure of the paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-135: In Section \ref{sec:problemmodeling , we present a 0-1 integer programming model for the PDP based on its definition and outline the steps to transform it into a Hamiltonian.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In Section \ref{sec:QAOA , we introduce the fundamental concept of QAOA.}
elements.sent-2411-137: In Section \ref{sec:Experiment , we employ a quantum simulator to solve the PDP using QAOA under various parameter combinations and provide a comprehensive analysis of the experimental results.}
elements.sent-2411-138: Finally, in Section \ref{sec:conclusion , we conclude the paper with a summary of the key findings.}
elements.sent-2411-139: The premise of using QAOA is to first map the objective function of a combinatorial optimization problem to the Hamiltonian of a quantum system.
elements.sent-2411-140: In this process, the Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) model often acts as a bridge.
elements.sent-2411-141: The standard approach involves transforming the original problem into, or directly modeling it as, a QUBO model, which is then converted into a Hamiltonian through variable substitution.
elements.sent-2411-142: Following this methodology, our approach sequentially transforms the PDP into a 0-1 integer programming model, a QUBO model, and finally a Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-143: We begin by presenting the 0-1 integer programming model for the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-144: Accordingly, the objective function in Eq. \ref{eq:pdptarget represents the total number of vertices in \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-145: Constraint \ref{eq:pdpc1 ensures that for each vertex \( i \), either \( i \) is in \( D \) or it has at least one neighbor in \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-146: This constraint guarantees that \( D \) forms a DS.
elements.sent-2411-147: For constraint \ref{eq:pdpc2 , the left-hand side calculates the number of edges in \( E \) where one endpoint belongs to \( V \setminus D \) and the other belongs to \( D \).}
elements.sent-2411-148: The right-hand side represents the number of vertices in \( V \setminus D \).
elements.sent-2411-149: Since \( D \) is a DS, every vertex in \( V \setminus D \) must have at least one neighbor in \( D \).
elements.sent-2411-150: Thus, the number of edges connecting \( V \setminus D \) to \( D \) must be at least \( |V| - \sum\limits_{i ^{|V| X_{i \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: When this inequality holds as an equality, it signifies that each vertex in \( V \setminus D \) has exactly one neighbor in \( D \), satisfying the perfect domination condition.
elements.sent-2411-152: Therefore, constraint \ref{eq:pdpc2 is the defining constraint of the PDP.}
elements.sent-2411-153: After formulating the 0-1 integer programming model for the PDP, the next step is to transform it into a QUBO model.
elements.sent-2411-154: The standard representation of the QUBO model is provided in Eq. \ref{eq:qubo , where the matrix \( Q \), commonly referred to as the QUBO matrix. \begin{equation}minimize/maximize \quad y = x^{t}Qx \label{eq:qubo}\end{equation} Given that \( X_{* \in \{0, 1\} \), it follows that \( X_{* = X_{* ^{2 \).}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-156: The next step involves transforming the constraints into quadratic penalty terms.

elements.sent-2411-160: For \( n \geq 3 \), slack variables must be introduced to convert the inequality in Eq. \ref{eq:ctnormal into an equality constraint, as shown in Eq. \ref{eq:scpdom . \begin{equation}X_{1} + X_{2} + \dots + X_{n} - S - 1 = 0 \label{eq:scpdom}\end{equation} It is evident that the range of \( S \) encompasses all integers within the interval \([0, n-1]\).}}

elements.sent-2411-168: The basic concept of QAOA is illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig:qaoaflow .}
elements.sent-2411-169: To begin, the spin operator and the Pauli operator are related as follows: \begin{equation}\hat{s} = \frac{\hbar}{2} \hat{\sigma}\end{equation} For the \( z \)-component of \( \hat{s \), it is naturally related to \( \hat{\sigma ^{z \).}}}
elements.sent-2411-170: In the Pauli representation, \begin{equation}\hat{\sigma}^{z} = \begin{pmatrix} 1 0 0 -1 \end{pmatrix}\end{equation} It has two eigenstates, \( \ket{0 = \begin{pmatrix 1 0 \end{pmatrix \) and \( \ket{1 = \begin{pmatrix 0 1 \end{pmatrix \), which correspond to two distinct spin directions.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Initially, we assume that each qubit in a quantum system with \( n \) qubits is in the \( \ket{0 \) state, such that the system's state is represented as \( \ket{\underbrace{00\ldots 0 _{n \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: Subsequently, QAOA applies the Hadamard gate to prepare this state into an equal superposition of all basis states, as described in Eq. \ref{eq:is .}
elements.sent-2411-173: Here, the bit string \( z = z_{1 z_{2 z_{3 \dots z_{n \), where \( z_{i \in \{0, 1\} \). \begin{equation}\ket{s} = \underbrace{\hat{H} \otimes \hat{H} \cdots \otimes \hat{H}}_{n} \ket{\underbrace{00\dots 0}_{n}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^{n}} } \cdot \sum\limits_{z} \ket{z} \label{eq:is}\end{equation} Next, QAOA applies two types of rotation operators, \( U(C, \gamma) \) and \( U(B, \beta) \) (Eq. \ref{eq:ug , Eq. \ref{eq:ub ), to the initial state \( \ket{s \), repeating the process \( q \) times.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-174: Here, \( C = H_c \), \( B = \sum\limits_{j=1 ^{n \sigma_{j ^{x \), with \( \gamma \in [0, 2\pi] \) and \( \beta \in [0, \pi] \).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-175: In Fig. \ref{fig:qaoaflow , \( \gamma_{q \) and \( \beta_{q \) denote the angles used in the \( q \)-th layer.}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: After \( q \) iterations, the final state \( \ket{\gamma, \beta \) is obtained, as shown in Eq. \ref{eq:gammabeta . \begin{equation}U(C,\gamma) = e^{-i\gamma H_{c}} \label{eq:ug} U(B,\beta) = e^{-i\beta B} \label{eq:ub}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\ket{\gamma,\beta} = U(B,\beta_{q})U(C,\gamma_{q}) \cdots U(B,\beta_{1})U(C,\gamma_{1}) \ket{s} \label{eq:gammabeta}\end{equation} With fixed values of \( \gamma \) and \( \beta \), by repeatedly executing the quantum circuit depicted in Fig. \ref{fig:qaoaflow and measuring the final state, the expectation value of \( H_{c \), denoted as \( F_{q (\gamma, \beta) \), can be obtained. \begin{equation}F_{q}(\gamma,\beta) = \bra{\gamma,\beta} H_{c} \ket{\gamma,\beta}\end{equation} Since the PDP is a minimization problem, the values of \( \gamma_{* \) and \( \beta_{* \) at each layer must be adjusted to minimize \( F_{q (\gamma, \beta) \).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: The essence of QAOA lies in applying two types of operators in a manner that increases the probability of the system collapsing into basis states corresponding to the smallest values of \( H_c \).
elements.sent-2411-178: As a hybrid algorithm, QAOA leverages classical optimization methods, such as COBYLA or Nelder-Mead, to tune the angles at each layer.
elements.sent-2411-179: The optimization process terminates when either the maximum number of iterations is reached or the function tolerance falls below a predefined threshold.
elements.sent-2411-180: Once the optimal values of \( \gamma_{* \) and \( \beta_{* \) are determined, the quantum circuit is updated accordingly, and multiple final samples are generated.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: The bit string \( z_{* \) with the highest probability from these samples is then output.}
elements.sent-2411-182: In \( z_{* \), the spin direction of each qubit corresponds to the 0-1 values of the decision variables in the original optimization problem, allowing for the direct extraction of the final PDS.}
elements.sent-2411-183: According to theoretical results \citep{RN436 , increasing the number of layers \( q \) brings \( F_{q (\gamma, \beta) \) closer to the optimal value.}}
elements.sent-2411-184: However, deeper layers lead to more complex quantum circuits, which present significant challenges when implementing QAOA on both real quantum computers and local quantum simulators.
elements.sent-2411-185: Therefore, this study focuses on analyzing the performance of QAOA in solving the PDP with a limited number of layers.

elements.sent-2411-244: In this paper, we explored the use of QAOA to solve the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-245: We began by modeling the PDP as a 0-1 integer programming problem based on its definition and transformed its two types of constraints into quadratic penalties to derive the QUBO model.
elements.sent-2411-246: Through variable substitution, we ultimately obtained the Hamiltonian for the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-247: Using IBM's Qiskit quantum simulator, we conducted extensive tests and analyses on the performance of QAOA in solving the PDP, testing 420 parameter combinations involving layer numbers, penalty coefficients, and maximum iterations.
elements.sent-2411-248: On a macro scale, QAOA successfully computed the correct PDS in 82 parameter combinations and identified the optimal PDS in 17 combinations, achieving a highest approximation ratio of approximately 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-249: These results confidently demonstrate that even with low-layer QAOA, excellent outcomes can be achieved with appropriately chosen parameters.
elements.sent-2411-250: This underscores the significant potential of quantum algorithms in solving the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-251: In the parameter analysis, we examined the parameter distributions for three categories of experiments: (1) those in the top 20\% for approximation ratio, (2) those where \( z_{* \) corresponds to the optimal PDS, and (3) those where \( z_{* \) corresponds to the correct PDS.}}
elements.sent-2411-252: The findings revealed distinct parameter tendencies between the first category and the latter two.
elements.sent-2411-253: Higher approximation ratios were associated with larger layer numbers and higher penalty coefficients for both constraints.
elements.sent-2411-254: In contrast, the latter two categories favored smaller layer structures and distinct penalty coefficient settings.
elements.sent-2411-255: These insights provide valuable guidance for optimizing parameter settings when applying QAOA to solve the PDP, paving the way for further advancements in quantum algorithm applications.
elements.sent-2411-256: The limitations of this paper include: (1) the algorithm was not tested on a real quantum computer, and (2) the quantum circuit was not optimized.
elements.sent-2411-257: All quantum circuits used in this study were generated using IBM's Qiskit.
elements.sent-2411-258: Based on the research content, experimental results, and limitations discussed in this paper, we propose the following directions for future work: (1) Evaluate the effectiveness of QAOA in solving the PDP on a real quantum computer.

elements.sent-2411-287: Perfect Domination Problem (PDP), a canonical challenge in combinatorial optimization, finds critical applications in real-world systems such as error-correcting codes, wireless communication networks, and social networks.
elements.sent-2411-288: Decades of research have firmly established its NP-completeness across numerous graph classes.
elements.sent-2411-289: Motivated by rapid advances in quantum computing, significant effort has recently been directed toward quantum algorithms for NP-complete problems, most notably the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA).
elements.sent-2411-290: Nonetheless, the applicability and efficacy of quantum approaches to the PDP remain entirely unexplored.
elements.sent-2411-291: This paper initiates the first systematic investigation of the PDP via QAOA.
elements.sent-2411-292: We evaluate solution quality on three benchmark instances of 6, 7, and 8 vertices using 15–18 qubits on a quantum simulator, examining more than 400 distinct parameter configurations.
elements.sent-2411-293: Experimental results confirm the algorithm’s effectiveness and expose discernible trends in parameter selection.
elements.sent-2411-294: These outcomes substantiate QAOA’s viability for the PDP and mark a seminal step toward situating this classical problem within the quantum-computing paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-295: For a graph \(G=(V,E)\), a dominating set (DS) is a subset \(D\subseteq V\) such that every vertex \(v\in V\setminus D\) is adjacent to at least one vertex in \(D\).
elements.sent-2411-296: The classical Domination Problem (DP) asks for a DS of minimum cardinality.
elements.sent-2411-297: A more restrictive criterion yields the perfect dominating set (PDS): here, every vertex \(v\in V\setminus D\) must be adjacent to exactly one vertex in \(D\).
elements.sent-2411-298: The Perfect Domination Problem (PDP) seeks a PDS of smallest possible size.
elements.sent-2411-299: Structurally, the PDP is a direct refinement of the DP; nevertheless, the two problems have distinct origins.
elements.sent-2411-300: The DP emerged from facility-location frameworks \citep{chang2013algorithmic , whereas the PDP arose as a graph theoretic generalisation of error-correcting codes in communication theory \citep{biggs1973perfect .}}
elements.sent-2411-301: Beyond these foundational contexts, the PDP finds wide-ranging applications in parallel computing architectures \citep{RN482 , wireless communication networks \citep{RN483 , and social network analysis \citep{RN485 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-302: From an algorithmic perspective, PDP has been proven NP-complete for many graph classes \citep{RN486, RN487 .}
elements.sent-2411-303: Over the years, numerous algorithms have been developed to address the PDP, often tailored to specific graph types or specialized problem variations, such as the weighted version.
elements.sent-2411-304: In recent years, the rapid advancement of quantum computing \citep{RN421, RN495, RN496, RN497 has spurred the development of numerous quantum algorithms aimed at addressing combinatorial optimization problems.}
elements.sent-2411-305: As one of the most influential quantum algorithms of the past decade, QAOA has been applied to a wide array of combinatorial optimization problems, including max-cut \citep{RN436 , the traveling salesman problem \citep{RN453 , the DP \citep{RN415,zhang2024quantum , max-flow \citep{RN420 , the minimum vertex cover problem \citep{RN334 , the boolean satisfiability problem\citep{RN455 , among others.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-306: Nevertheless, as a pivotal problem bridging communications and combinatorics, PDP has, to date, not been approached by any quantum algorithm; investigations into quantum algorithms for this problem remain entirely absent.
elements.sent-2411-307: Against this backdrop, we investigate the deployment of the QAOA for the resolution of PDP.
elements.sent-2411-308: Employing 14–18 qubits within a quantum simulator, we successfully solved PDP on graphs of varying scales.
elements.sent-2411-309: The principal contributions of this work are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-310: The resulting data elucidate the response patterns and performance tendencies of QAOA when applied to PDP, thereby furnishing critical insights and concrete guidance for subsequent research.

elements.sent-2411-364: Using IBM's Qiskit quantum simulator, we conducted extensive tests and analyses on the performance of QAOA in solving the PDP.
elements.sent-2411-365: In basic testing, we tested 420 parameter combinations involving layer numbers, penalty coefficients, and maximum iterations.
elements.sent-2411-366: In the parameter analysis, we primarily examined the parameter distributions for three categories of experiments: (1) those in the top 20\% for approximation ratio, (2) those where \( z_{* \) corresponds to the optimal PDS, and (3) those where \( z_{* \) corresponds to the correct PDS.}}
elements.sent-2411-367: Pursuing a high approximation ratio focuses on the advantage of relative probability, in which case there is a tendency to increase the penalty coefficient for the perfect condition.
elements.sent-2411-368: In contrast, the other two metrics reflect the advantage of global probability, where distinct penalty coefficient settings are more preferable, and QAOA with a small number of layers is better suited for traditional optimization algorithms to optimize the global probability under the current test cases.
elements.sent-2411-369: Subsequent analysis results on the differences in optimization landscapes under different ratios of penalty coefficients have also confirmed this conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-370: In addition to the 6-vertex graph, we also tested slightly larger graphs with 7 and 8 vertices.
elements.sent-2411-371: It can be observed that under the given parameters, the QAOA-based method can compute the optimal PDS in all cases.
elements.sent-2411-372: Although the order of magnitude of the probability of obtaining the optimal solution decreases, the advantage of the sampling probability of the optimal solution in the global context is quite evident.
elements.sent-2411-373: These results confirm the effectiveness of this method.These insights provide valuable guidance for optimizing parameter settings when applying QAOA to solve the PDP, paving the way for further advancements in quantum algorithm applications.
elements.sent-2411-374: The limitations of this paper include: (1) Due to the substantial computational resources required for local quantum simulations, and given that modeling the PDP for an 8-vertex graph already approaches 18 qubits, this paper is currently unable to test larger-scale instances.
